title: Dog Straining to Defecate: Causes, Signs, and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your dog straining with no result? Learn about tenesmus, why it is a medium-urgency concern, and how to help your vet triage the situation.
content_html:
TL;DR: Tenesmus is straining to defecate and requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours to rule out causes like blockages or inflammation. Providing photos of your dog’s posture and any passed material helps the clinic accurately triage and prioritize your pet.

What is tenesmus and what causes my dog to strain while defecating?

  • Tenesmus is the medical term for when your dog repeatedly tries to defecate with little to no success.
  • It is a common sign of discomfort in the lower gastrointestinal tract.
  • While often mistaken for simple constipation, it can also be caused by colitis, intestinal blockages, or urinary obstructions.

How urgent is it if my dog is straining to pass stool?

  • This condition is a Medium Urgency level and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 hours.
  • While not an immediate life-threatening crisis like bloat, it should not be left for several days.
  • Prompt medical attention prevents secondary issues like rectal prolapse, dehydration, or extreme pain.

Why should I take a photo or video of my dog straining for the vet?

  • Capture a photo or short video of your dog's posture and any passed material, such as mucus, blood, or liquid.
  • Visual evidence helps the medical team distinguish between true constipation and "pseudodiarrhea" caused by inflammation.
  • These visuals help ensure your dog is prioritized correctly during the clinic triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Straining to defecate in dogs can be caused by several factors, including compression of the colon or rectum from a narrowed pelvic inlet (due to improperly healed pelvic fractures), enlarged sublumbar lymph nodes, or an enlarged prostate gland. Colonic stricture due to trauma or neoplasia should also be considered. Other diseases affecting neuromuscular control of the colon and rectum, such as hypothyroidism, dysautonomia, and spinal cord lesions, can contribute. Hypokalemia and hypercalcemia can also adversely affect muscular control. Certain drugs (e.g., opioids, diuretics, antihistamines, anticholinergic agents, sucralfate, aluminum hydroxide, potassium bromide, and calcium channel-blocking agents) may promote constipation. Extraluminal constriction may be caused by neoplasia, enlarged prostate, and scar tissue after perianal fistula or anal sac abscess. Examination should be performed to determine the presence of perineal hernia, foreign material, pain, or masses. Plain abdominal radiographs may help establish the inciting factors of fecal retention. A CBC, biochemical profile including a serum T4 level, and urinalysis may be warranted in chronic cases.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: Could my dog be straining due to a urinary issue?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dogs, particularly males, may look like they are straining to defecate when they actually have a life-threatening urinary blockage. If you haven't seen your dog produce urine in 12 hours, treat it as an immediate emergency.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog a human laxative?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications, including laxatives or stool softeners, without a veterinarian's direct recommendation. Some human products can cause severe dehydration or electrolyte imbalances in pets.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of straining?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dietary indiscretion (eating something they shouldn't), dehydration, lack of fiber, intestinal parasites, or swallowing a foreign object that has caused a partial blockage.
